skill.name: azure-communication-chat-java
skill.description: Build Java chat features with Azure Communication Services Chat SDK, including threads, messages, participants, read receipts, and typing notifications.
skill.summary: Guide Java developers through Azure Communication Services Chat SDK implementation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and ordinary documentation text. The hardcoded URL findings are Azure endpoint placeholders for legitimate SDK configuration, with no evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malware intent, or executable install scripts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Formatting Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged fenced code blocks and inline backtick formatting as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed lines are documentation examples for Maven, Java SDK usage, Bash environment variables, and inline Java class names; they are not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are Markdown fences or inline code spans in SKILL.md. No script file, shell invocation, dynamic command construction, or execution instruction is present.

low_findings.title: Azure Endpoint Placeholder in SDK Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to placeholder Azure Communication Services endpoints used to show normal ChatClient configuration. This indicates network use by the Azure SDK, but there is no evidence of unauthorized external transmission or hidden endpoint behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints are visible placeholders for the documented Azure service. The skill does not include executable code that sends secrets to an unrelated domain.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Unsubstantiated
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak-cryptography alerts at the cited lines do not correspond to a cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic API usage. Line 3 is descriptive metadata, and line 282 is a Markdown table header.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain no DES, MD5, SHA1, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic implementation. The finding appears to be a scanner pattern mismatch.

content.user_title: Build Azure Chat Apps in Java
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable examples for Azure Communication Services chat features. This skill provides focused guidance for threads, messages, participants, receipts, and typing notifications.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure Communication Chat Java SDK.
Explains ChatClient and ChatThreadClient creation with token credentials.
Provides examples for creating threads, sending messages, and listing messages.
Covers participant management, read receipts, typing notifications, and topic updates.
Includes pagination and basic HTTP error-handling patterns for chat operations.
limitations: Does not issue Azure user access tokens or manage identity creation.
Does not provide a complete frontend chat user interface.
Does not replace Azure security, retention, or compliance configuration.
Examples use placeholders and must be adapted for production secrets handling.

use_cases.title: Add Chat to a Java Application
use_cases.description: Use the skill to wire Azure chat clients, create threads, send messages, and handle thread-level operations.

use_cases.title: Design User Messaging Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the skill to plan participant history, read receipts, typing indicators, and system message behavior.

use_cases.title: Review Azure Chat SDK Usage
use_cases.description: Use the skill to compare implementation code against common SDK classes, options, and operational patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Chat Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to configure the Azure Communication Services Chat Java SDK for a basic backend chat client.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Thread Messaging
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Java flow that creates a chat thread, adds participants, sends a message, and stores the thread ID.

prompt_templates.title: Add Receipts and Typing Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through adding read receipts and typing notifications to an existing Azure Communication Services Java chat service.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Chat Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Azure Communication Services Chat Java implementation for pagination, token refresh, participant history, and error handling gaps.

output_examples.input: Create a Java chat thread with two participants.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step implementation plan that identifies the required SDK classes, participant setup, thread creation call, and stored thread identifier.

output_examples.input: Add read receipts and typing notifications to chat.
output_examples.output: Guidance that explains when to send receipts, how to list receipt records, and how to trigger typing notifications from the thread client.

output_examples.input: Improve error handling for message sending.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering unauthorized tokens, missing thread membership, missing threads, and default handling for unexpected HTTP responses.

best_practices: Use short-lived user tokens and implement refresh logic outside the skill examples.
Set participant history sharing deliberately when adding users to existing threads.
Paginate message lists and filter system messages before rendering user chat views.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real user tokens or Azure resource endpoints in source code.
Do not expose all previous thread history to newly added participants by default.
Do not send read receipts until the user has actually viewed the message.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure Communication Services resources?
faq.answer: No. It documents Java SDK usage after an Azure Communication Services resource and user token already exist.

faq.question: Can it help with synchronous and asynchronous clients?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows both ChatClient and ChatAsyncClient creation patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover participant management?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for listing, adding, and removing chat participants.

faq.question: Does it include real-time event handling?
faq.answer: It covers typing notifications and read receipts, but it does not provide a complete event-driven frontend.

faq.question: Is this safe for production code as written?
faq.answer: No. The examples use placeholders and need production token handling, logging controls, and access checks.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
